A reputation for piety and honesty is more desirable than all the wealth and pleasure in this world. It will do more good to go to a funeral than to a feast. We may lawfully go to both, as there is occasion; our Savior both feasted at the wedding of his friend in Cana and wept at the grave of his friend in Bethany. But, considering how apt we are to be vain and indulge the flesh, it is best to go to the house of mourning to learn the end of man as to this world. Seriousness is better than mirth and jollity. That which is best for us is also best for our souls, though it may be unpleasing to our senses. It is better to have our corruptions mortified by the rebuke of the wise than to have them gratified by the song of fools. The laughter of a fool is soon gone; the end of his mirth is heaviness.

The outcome of our trials and difficulties is often better than we first thought. Surely it is better to be patient in spirit than to be proud and hasty. Do not be easily angered, nor quick to resent an affront. Do not remain angry; though anger may come into the heart of a wise man, it passes through him as a way-faring man; it dwells only in the hearts of fools. It is foolish to complain about the badness of our times when we have more reason to lament the badness of our own hearts; and even in these times we enjoy many mercies. It is foolish to praise the goodness of former times, as if former ages had not the same things to complain of that we do; this arises from discontent and a tendency to quarrel with God himself.

Wisdom is as good as an inheritance, indeed better. It shelters from the storms and scorching heat of trouble. Wealth will not lengthen out our natural life, but true wisdom will give spiritual life and strengthen people for service under their sufferings. Let us view the circumstances of our lives as the work of God, and in the end all will appear to have been for the best. In acts of righteousness, do not be carried into extremes or passions, no, not even by a zeal for God. Do not be conceited about your own abilities; nor find fault with everything, nor busy yourself in other people’s matters. Many who will not be influenced by the fear of God and the dread of hell will avoid sins that ruin their health and estate and expose them to public justice. But those who truly fear God have only one end to serve, therefore act steadily. If we say we have not sinned, we deceive ourselves. Every true believer is ready to say, ‘God be merciful to me a sinner.’ Do not forget, at the same time, that personal righteousness, walking in newness of life, is the only real evidence of an interest by faith in the righteousness of the Redeemer. Wisdom teaches us not to be quick in resenting affronts. Do not desire to know what people say; if they speak well of you, it will feed your pride, and if ill, it will stir up your passion. See that you approve yourself to God and your own conscience, and then do not heed what men say about you; it is easier to pass by twenty affronts than to avenge one. When harm is done to us, examine whether we have not done as bad to others.

Solomon, in his search into the nature and reason of things, had been miserably deluded. But he here speaks with godly sorrow. He alone who constantly aims to please God can expect to escape; the careless sinner will probably fall and rise no more. He now discovered more than ever the evil of the great sin of which he had been guilty, loving many strange women. He did not find a woman thoroughly upright and godly. How was he likely to find such a one among those he had collected? If any of them had been well disposed, their situation would tend to make them all nearly of the same character. He here warns others against the sins into which he had been betrayed. Many a godly man can with thankfulness acknowledge that he has found a prudent, virtuous woman in the wife of his bosom; but those men who have followed Solomon’s example cannot expect to find one. He traces up all the streams of actual transgression to the fountain. It is clear that man is corrupted and revolted, and not as he was made. It is lamentable that man, whom God made upright, has found out so many ways to render himself wicked and miserable. Let us bless Him for Jesus Christ and seek his grace, that we may be numbered with his chosen people.
